title: Author tests for ProjectHub visibility regressions and unsafe auxiliary surfaces

ground_truth_issues.description: A test chain proves a standard participant cannot enumerate, retrieve, mutate, or destroy resources that belong to a different organisational boundary, while an equivalent same-boundary flow still succeeds.

A test chain proves non-privileged callers do not receive privileged metadata or internal collaboration content through primary responses or secondary packaging flows; seeded marker values must remain absent, while privileged callers retain their broader view.

A test chain proves delegated access remains confined to the explicitly granted workspace, cannot be replayed or stretched to neighbouring resources, and fails once the delegated state is invalid, expired, or incomplete.

A test chain proves diagnostic, historical, or operational surfaces cannot be used by non-privileged callers to retrieve broad application state, credentials, configuration, or organisation-wide records, while intended privileged access still works.

A table-driven test chain proves crafted inputs across discovery, reporting, outbound-request, and file-retrieval surfaces cannot exfiltrate hidden data or trigger unsafe behaviour, while benign variants on those same surfaces still work.

A test chain proves a non-privileged caller cannot overwrite ownership, boundary placement, or privileged attributes through ordinary update paths, and confirms the authoritative state with follow-up reads from more than one surface.
A test verifies delegated-access tokens and shareable links are unique, non-derivable from stable identifiers, and not reused across repeated issuances or different actors.

A test verifies archived, mirrored, private, or otherwise non-default visibility modes do not leak into unrelated callers' list, discovery, or packaged views, while their intended audiences still retain access.
A test verifies non-privileged callers cannot recover internal discussions or restricted file content through collaboration views, file retrieval, or alternate parent-child lookup paths.
A test verifies rollup, summary, or widening-style surfaces do not expand a caller's visibility to organisation-wide figures, internal metrics, or other teams' history.
A test verifies specialised roles with narrower read permissions remain confined to their documented visibility slice and do not become general cross-organisational readers.
A test chain triggers malformed or failing requests and verifies error responses do not expose internal runtime details, filesystem hints, or executable command text.

Anti-overblock tests prove legitimate privileged and same-boundary flows remain available after fixes, including broader administrative visibility, same-team leadership actions, and ordinary participant work management.
Tests isolate state with fresh setup or unique markers so results are repeatable and not order-dependent.
Tests use body-level assertions with unique sentinel markers and reusable helpers, rather than relying only on status codes or field-key absence.
